title: Weekly links July 17: African soccer migrants, the public-sector’s impacts on private hiring, rural markets from space, and more…
gist: A weekly links roundup covering how public-sector job amenities in Côte d'Ivoire raise private-sector hiring costs economy-wide, a new satellite-imaging method for detecting rural African markets, evidence that just three funders (J-PAL, World Bank, FCDO) anchor a highly dispersed RCT funding landscape, and economists' personal accounts of balancing motherhood with academic careers.

themes.theme: Public-sector amenities raise private hiring costs (Côte d'Ivoire)

insights.claim: In research on Côte d'Ivoire graduates' job preferences, Esther Girsberger and Romuald Méango find that non-wage amenities offered by the public sector — job security and shorter working hours — make it costly for the private sector to attract equally qualified workers.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific labor-market friction (public-sector non-wage benefits) that structurally inflates private-sector hiring costs in Côte d'Ivoire.
insights.locator: Esther Girsberger and Romuald Méango report on work in Côte d'Ivoire

insights.claim: Improvements in perceived job security alone can increase the perceived value of a public-sector job by around one-fifth of average wages, while the perceived value of shorter public-sector working hours corresponds to around 10% of the average wage.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Quantifies the non-wage compensation premium (roughly 30% of wages combined) the private sector must overcome to compete for talent against public employment.
insights.quote: improvements in job security alone can increase the perceived value of a public sector job by around one-fifth of average wages for much of the population, while the perceived value of lower working hours in the public sector corresponds to around 10% of the average wage
insights.locator: Most associate the public sector with 'stable' and 'secure' jobs

insights.claim: Because attracting the marginal worker away from the public sector requires compensating not just wages but also job security, working conditions, and perceived risk, the public sector effectively raises labor costs economy-wide in Côte d'Ivoire.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: A macro-level implication: large public-sector employment can be a hidden tax on private-sector competitiveness via labor-cost inflation, relevant to EM labor-market and public-employment-reform analysis.
insights.quote: hiring additional workers becomes progressively more expensive, and that the public sector effectively raises labour costs economy-wide
insights.locator: hiring additional workers becomes progressively more expensive

themes.theme: Satellite detection of rural market activity

insights.claim: Tillman von Carnap and Anna Tompsett developed a method to measure rural market activity using satellite imaging, exploiting the fact that periodic markets look visually distinct on market days versus non-market days, creating a unique temporal signature that allows detection without any other location information.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: A scalable, low-cost remote-sensing method for mapping informal rural market activity across Africa without ground surveys — relevant for agri-market infrastructure targeting and data vendors.
insights.quote: Our approach relies on periodic markets looking very different on market days and non-market days… These periodic appearance changes create a unique temporal signature that allows us to detect the presence of markets even without any other information about their locations.
insights.locator: Tillman von Carnap and Anna Tompsett discuss how to measure rural market activity

themes.theme: Concentration and dispersion in RCT funding

insights.claim: Jessica Leight's analysis of 392 published RCTs finds that virtually all (360) report a funder, spanning 612 distinct funders, with J-PAL, the World Bank, and FCDO and its funded research networks playing a leading role, but overall funding sources remain highly dispersed with limited scope for dominance by a single funder.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Maps the concentration structure of development-economics RCT funding — useful for understanding institutional influence and potential funder bias in the published evidence base.
insights.quote: Within the 392 RCTs, virtually all (360) report a funder, and there is high dispersion (612 distinct funders).
insights.locator: Jessica Leight on which are the main funders for RCTs

insights.claim: 82% of African players at the World Cup play for clubs overseas, a data point the Africa Jobs Fund substack uses to discuss migration and 'brain gain' dynamics, with soccer players cited as one example of a broader pattern.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: A concrete, highly visible data point (82% overseas) for framing African skilled-labor emigration ('brain gain vs. brain drain') debates beyond the usual professional/technical sectors.
insights.locator: the Africa Jobs Fund substack has a post entitled

themes.theme: Motherhood and the academic career track

insights.claim: Economist Barbara Biasi describes fearing, despite relatively smooth pregnancies and quick returns to work, that colleagues would see her as less committed or ambitious because she had children as an assistant professor, and describes how motherhood ultimately changed her relationship to professional setbacks, making her less reactive to rejection.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: A first-person data point on perceived career-penalty fears among women academics around childbearing, relevant to gender-equity policy in research institutions.
insights.quote: in spite of these relatively smooth rides and quick returns, it took me a long time to stop fearing that people would take me less seriously because I had chosen to have children while an assistant professor.
insights.locator: the most recent posts are 1) from Barbara Biasi

insights.claim: Development economist Jessica Leight describes travel constraints as a major challenge of parenting while conducting field-based development research, and identifies carving out cognitive space for ambitious, longer-term plans (versus routine task management) as her most significant ongoing challenge.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Illustrates a structural constraint (field-travel incompatibility with caregiving) specific to development economics as a discipline, relevant to talent-pipeline and research-capacity discussions.
insights.quote: Parenting crowds your brain, and as I'm now learning, it only becomes more crowded as they grow. The best benefit that any parental support policy can provide is a bit of space to un-crowd it.
insights.locator: and 2) from Jessica Leight who discusses two key challenges
